bullets.ppt_text: Model capacity and data capacity should be matched to prevent overfitting and underfitting.
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher explained that model capacity refers to the ability to approximate a wide variety of functions — a linear model is low capacity because it struggles with nonlinear patterns, while a highly polynomial model is high capacity and can even memorize random noise. The classic example given was a dataset generated by y = x² + noise: fitting a straight line leads to underfitting, while fitting a very high-degree polynomial leads to overfitting. The key insight is that if you have a small dataset you should use a simpler model, and if your data is complex you need a higher-capacity model — mismatch in either direction causes problems. This matters because getting the capacity match right is the foundation of good generalization performance.

The teacher linked underfitting to high bias (the model is too simple and cannot capture the true pattern) and overfitting to high variance (the model memorizes training data including noise, creating a large gap between training and validation loss). The bias/variance trade-off was illustrated with a U-shaped generalization loss curve as a function of model complexity: training loss keeps falling as complexity grows, but validation loss first falls then rises, so the sweet spot is where the gap between the two is minimized. The teacher noted that a small, inevitable gap is acceptable — the goal is to minimize validation loss, not eliminate the gap entirely. Understanding this relationship tells you which direction to adjust your model when something goes wrong.

bullets.ppt_text: Empirically, use training/validation error to evaluate
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher described three characteristic patterns visible in a loss-vs-epoch progress board: (1) the desired case — both training and validation loss decrease steadily with only a small gap between them; (2) underfitting — the loss barely decreases at all over epochs, signaling a fundamental model problem; (3) overfitting — training loss decreases steadily but a large gap opens up between training and validation loss. Reading these curves is the practical skill that lets you diagnose what is wrong without guessing. The teacher emphasized trial-and-error: look at the progress board first, then decide which direction to adjust.

bullets.ppt_text: Know what to do under which circumstance
bullets.ai_comment: The teacher gave a concrete action plan for each diagnosis: if you see high bias (underfitting), train longer, use a bigger neural network, or change the model architecture to accommodate the data complexity. If you see high variance (overfitting — large training/validation gap), add more data, use architecture search to find a better structure, or apply regularization techniques such as weight decay (L2 penalty on the loss) or dropout. The teacher also warned that in real projects it is common to face both high bias and high variance simultaneously, and the minimum acceptable target is to at least reach pure overfitting (which is easier to fix) rather than staying stuck with both problems at once. This practical roadmap is directly applicable to the final project.
